claim: Netlify deploy previews use a fixed URL pattern deploy-preview-{PR_NUMBER}--{site-name}.netlify.app and are auto-created when a PR is opened against a connected GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket/Azure DevOps repo.
quote: Deploy Previews work by deploying pull/merge requests to a unique URL different from the one your production site uses, with the URL having the prefix deploy-preview followed by the identifier number of the pull request or merge request.

claim: Vercel automatically generates an isolated, ephemeral preview deployment with a unique URL on every push to a non-production branch or when a PR is opened.
quote: Vercel creates a preview deployment when you push a commit to a branch that is not your production branch or create a pull request (PR) on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. Preview environments allow you to deploy and test changes in a live setting, without affecting your production site.

claim: Cloudflare Pages issues both a hash-based preview URL and a branch-name alias (e.g. fix-api.<project>.pages.dev) for each PR, with the alias re-pointing to the latest commit.
quote: Every time you open a new pull request on your GitHub repository, Cloudflare Pages will create a unique preview URL, which will stay updated as you continue to push new commits to the branch.

claim: Render preview environments clone the full production blueprint (services, databases, env groups) per PR and tear them down automatically when the PR is merged or closed.
quote: Render keeps your preview environments up to date on every commit and automatically destroys them when the original pull request is merged or closed.

claim: Fly.io implements review apps via a GitHub Action that creates, deploys, and destroys per-PR Fly apps at pr-<PR>-<owner>-<repo>.fly.dev.
quote: By default, review apps will be available at https://pr-<PR number>-<repository owner>-<repository name>.fly.dev.

claim: Coolify, a self-hosted PaaS, builds the same per-PR pattern using a wildcard DNS record and a configurable subdomain template ({{pr_id}} or {{random}}), tearing down on PR merge/close.
quote: If set to true, all PR's that are opened against the resource's configured branch, will be deployed to a unique URL.

claim: Dokploy mirrors the same model on self-hosted infra: a PR opens, a preview is built, Dokploy comments the URL on the PR, and label-based filtering is offered.
quote: Once enabled, preview deployments are automatically created whenever a pull request is opened against your target branch... Dokploy will automatically comment on the PR with a preview link.

claim: The value proposition is shifting review left: PMs, designers, and QA can click a URL and exercise the running build before merge instead of waiting on staging or local setup.
quote: Preview environments make code reviewable by non-engineers. When a designer, product manager, or QA engineer can click a URL and see the feature running in a real environment, they do not have to wait for a staging deployment or ask an engineer to set something up locally.

claim: Teams adopt preview environments to eliminate last-minute surprises and shorten the feedback loop so issues are caught while still cheap to fix.
quote: Preview environments eliminate last-minute surprises by spinning up production-like copies for every feature branch — giving PMs, QA, and stakeholders early access to test changes before merge.

claim: Netlify popularised the term 'Deploy Preview' in 2016, framing it as automatic per-PR builds posted back to the PR for reviewer feedback — the template most other vendors copied.
quote: Introducing Deploy Previews in Netlify

claim: Container Manager replaced the Docker package in DSM 7.2 and introduced a Project tab for native Docker Compose management directly in the GUI.
quote: Container Manager was released with DSM 7.2 as the successor to the Docker package... The Project tab represents a new improvement in DSM 7.2 that enables creation and management of containerized applications via Docker Compose files.

claim: Container Manager 24.0.2-1535 upgraded the Docker engine from 20.10 to 24.0.2, but locks core container settings (ports, volumes, env, links) once created — users must duplicate to modify.
quote: settings for containers—including ports, volumes, environments, and links—cannot be modified post-creation... The update upgraded Docker from version 20.10 to 24.0.2

claim: DSM 7.2 Container Manager is officially limited to x86_64 Diskstations; aarch64/ARM models require community workarounds.
quote: Container Manager is only available on Diskstations with x86_64 architecture CPU

claim: Community packages exist to install Container Manager on ARMv8 Synology models (DS118, DS218, DS418, etc.) that Synology does not officially support.
quote: Container Manager for DS118, DS218, DS218play, DS418, DS418j, DS119j and RS819

claim: DSM 7's built-in reverse proxy lives in Control Panel → Login Portal → Advanced → Reverse Proxy, and accepts source/destination protocol, hostname (FQDN) and port — no path-based routing in the UI.
quote: The reverse proxy feature is accessed via Control Panel > Login Portal > Advanced > Reverse Proxy... only fully qualified domain names (FQDN) are accepted—formats like 'yourdomainame.com/web1' are invalid.

claim: WebSocket support is added per-rule via Custom Header → WebSocket, which auto-injects two header rows; many app containers (Portainer, Home Assistant, Mealie, Pi-Hole) require this to work behind the reverse proxy.
quote: click the Custom Header tab, click Create and then from the drop-down menu click WebSocket, and two Header Names and two Values will be automatically added

claim: The DSM reverse proxy UI is limited to per-subdomain routing — there is no app-subfolder (/app) configuration in the dialog, so wildcard or path-based fan-out requires manual nginx tweaks.
quote: for RP dialog box you can't configure it to work with app subfolder (/app), just as a subdomain

claim: Web Station in DSM 7.2 supports both Nginx (default) and Apache 2.4 as HTTP back-ends, multiple PHP runtimes (7.3–8.2), HTTP/2, HSTS, and per-portal SSL certificates.
quote: Web Station offers two HTTP server options: Nginx and Apache HTTP Server version 2.4... PHP 7.3, PHP 7.4, PHP 8.0, PHP 8.1, PHP 8.2... HTTP/2, HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)

claim: DSM 7 Web Station replaces the term 'virtual host' with Web Service / Web Portal entries, where each portal selects its own HTTP back-end and certificate.
quote: Web Portal | Web Station - Synology Knowledge Center

claim: DSM 7.2 introduced a regression where Apache 2.4 virtual hosts dropped the Options directive (MultiViews, FollowSymLinks, ExecCGI), requiring a manual edit of apache24_service_template.mustache.
quote: After updating to DSM 7.2, virtual hosts lost the Options directive containing MultiViews FollowSymLinks ExecCGI in their Directory blocks

claim: On DSM 7, SSH key auth requires correct ownership and permissions (home 755, .ssh 755, authorized_keys 644) — the default home permission of 777 causes sshd to silently reject keys; sshd reload is `sudo systemctl reload sshd`.
quote: Home directory (/volume1/homes/{username}): 755... .ssh directory: 755... authorized_keys file: 644... The default home directory permission of 777 causes the SSH daemon to reject key authentication

claim: Only members of the administrators group may SSH to DSM by default, and DSM admin rights are distinct from sudo rights — passwordless sudo needs an explicit NOPASSWD line in /etc/sudoers.
quote: $USER ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL

claim: DSM Task Scheduler can run user-defined shell scripts on cron-like schedules under any system user (root by default) and can email stdout/stderr after each run.
quote: Task Scheduler | DSM - Synology Knowledge Center

claim: Task Scheduler User-defined Script entries take an absolute script path (e.g. /volume1/scripts/script.sh) and execute as the configured owner — the practical hook for periodic preview-environment cleanup.
quote: In the box under User-defined script type the path to the script, for example: /volume1/scripts/script_name.sh

claim: Synology storage pools on Btrfs provide copy-on-write snapshots usable for cheap per-preview rollbacks on NAS-class hosts.
quote: How Btrfs protects your company's data | Synology Inc.

claim: Snapshot Replication requires Btrfs volumes — entry-level J-series models (e.g. DS220J) ship ext4-only and cannot use this path for preview state restore.
quote: Snapshot Replication

claim: DSM's built-in Let's Encrypt client only supports the HTTP-01 challenge, which cannot issue wildcard certificates and requires port 80 open to the internet.
quote: If your NAS is not connected to the Internet, you have multiple web servers, you don't want to/can't open port 80 or you want to use wildcard certificates, you would need to use the DNS-01 challenge instead.

claim: acme.sh ships an official synology_dsm deploy hook that imports the issued cert via DSM's web API using SYNO_Username, SYNO_Password, SYNO_Scheme, SYNO_Port, SYNO_Certificate and SYNO_Create env vars.
quote: SYNO_Username ... SYNO_Password ... SYNO_Certificate ... SYNO_Scheme ... SYNO_Port ... SYNO_Create=1 ... ./acme.sh --deploy --home . -d "$CERT_DOMAIN" --deploy-hook synology_dsm

claim: A working DSM 7.2 recipe issues both apex and wildcard in one cert and deploys it with --insecure on first run because no valid cert exists yet on the localhost DSM endpoint.
quote: sudo docker-compose exec acme.sh --issue --dns dns_cf -d "mydomain.com" -d "*.mydomain.com" ... sudo docker-compose exec acme.sh --deploy --insecure -d domain.com --deploy-hook synology_dsm --debug

claim: Let's Encrypt allows 50 certs per registered domain per week and 5 duplicate certs per week, which is the relevant ceiling if you avoid wildcards and issue one cert per PR subdomain instead.
quote: Up to 50 certificates can be issued per registered domain ... Duplicate Certificate limit of 5 per week

claim: Cloudflare supports wildcard DNS records on all plans, both proxied and unproxied (DNS-only); only A, AAAA and CNAME record types can be proxied.
quote: Cloudflare now supports proxying wildcard '*' record for DNS management in all customer plans ... Only records used for IP address resolution — A, AAAA, and CNAME records — can be proxied.

claim: cloudflared ingress accepts a leading-asterisk wildcard hostname (e.g. *.preview.example.com) that routes any subdomain to one local origin, removing the need for port-forwarding or a public wildcard cert.
quote: if you set the hostname key to *.example.com, both alpha.example.com and beta.example.com will route traffic to your origin ... cloudflared does not support wildcards in the middle of the hostname

claim: Caddy's On-Demand TLS provisions a per-host Let's Encrypt cert during the first TLS handshake for an unknown SNI, removing the need to know PR subdomains in advance, but it must be paired with an 'ask' endpoint to prevent abuse and is rate-limited internally to 10 attempts per ACME account per 10 seconds.
quote: dynamically obtains a new certificate during the first TLS handshake that requires it ... must be both enabled and restricted to prevent abuse ... 10 attempts per ACME account per 10 seconds

claim: An alternative Python-based wrapper exists specifically for renewing wildcard Let's Encrypt certs on Synology via DNS-01 and importing them through the DSM API, packaged as a scheduled task.
quote: Python script for automatically renewing Let's Encrypt certificates on Synology NAS using DNS-01 challenge. Also supports wildcard certificates.

claim: DSM 7 specific community walkthroughs note that the 'Improve protection against cross-site request forgery' setting must be enabled in Control Panel > Security for the synology_dsm deploy hook to succeed.
quote: The "Improve protection against cross-site request forgery attacks" setting must be enabled in DSM's Control Panel > Security > Security for the deploy-hook to work.

claim: appleboy/ssh-action (6.1k stars, v1.2.5 Jan 2026) executes remote SSH commands from GitHub Actions; supports password, RSA/ED25519 keys, key passphrases, host fingerprint verification, and proxy/jump-host hops.
quote: GitHub Actions for executing remote ssh commands. ... Supports password, key, key with passphrase, host fingerprint verification, proxy/jump host.

claim: easingthemes/ssh-deploy (1.3k stars, v6.0.1 Apr 2026) wraps rsync over SSH from GITHUB_WORKSPACE to a remote target with pre/post scripts; the SSH private key must have no passphrase because rsync ssh cannot prompt for one.
quote: Private key part of an SSH key pair... You should not set a Passphrase (keep it empty) for the private key you generated. Because rsync ssh (used for deploy) does not support private key password to be entered as a command line parameter.

claim: Synology Container Manager cannot authenticate to GHCR via its UI ('Registry returned bad result'); the standard workaround is to SSH into DSM and run `sudo docker pull ghcr.io/<org>/<image>:<tag>` so the image lands in the local Docker daemon Container Manager reads.
quote: Synology Container Manager does not support the GitHub Container Registry's token authentication ... sudo docker pull ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main

claim: A self-hosted runner can be installed on Synology by symlinking /var/run/docker.sock into /volume1/docker, deploying myoung34/github-runner via docker-compose, and re-creating the symlink on boot via Task Scheduler; the author warns to use it only for private repositories.
quote: ln -s /var/run/docker.sock /volume1/docker/docker.sock ... you should only use it with private repositories to have full control over the running code.

claim: GitHub explicitly warns that self-hosted runners should almost never be used for public repositories because any forker who can open a PR can run a malicious workflow on the runner and exfiltrate secrets and the GITHUB_TOKEN.
quote: Self-hosted runners should almost never be used for public repositories on GitHub, because any user can open pull requests against the repository and compromise the environment.

claim: The poutine static-analysis rule `pr_runs_on_self_hosted` flags any workflow triggered by `pull_request` that runs on a self-hosted runner because a fork PR can inject a new workflow under attacker control.
quote: an adversary can create a pull request with a new malicious workflow where 'runs-on' is set to the self-hosted runner label, and this malicious workflow can use 'pull_request' as the event target, thereby it will run immediately once the pull request is created.

claim: The official tailscale/github-action@v4 uses an OAuth client (TS_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID/SECRET) plus a tag (e.g. tag:ci) to bring up an ephemeral, auto-logout tailnet node inside the runner so subsequent SSH/rsync steps can reach a NAS behind NAT without a port-forward.
quote: When your workflow runs, it uses the federated identity and a GitHub JWT identity token to create an ephemeral node ... uses: tailscale/github-action@v4 with: oauth-client-id ... tags: tag:ci

claim: Tailscale runs on Synology DSM as a package, but Tailscale SSH itself is unsupported on the NAS, so the recommended pattern is to authenticate with the DSM-built-in OpenSSH server reachable on the tailnet IP/MagicDNS name.
quote: Tailscale SSH does not run on Synology, but you can use the SSH server provided in DSM instead.

claim: An end-to-end working pattern: Cloudflare Tunnel exposes Portainer to the internet behind Zero-Trust service-token Access policy; the GitHub Actions job sends `curl -X POST $PORTAINER_WEBHOOK_URL` with `CF-Access-Client-Id` and `CF-Access-Client-Secret` headers to redeploy.
quote: curl -X POST "$PORTAINER_WEBHOOK_URL" -H "CF-Access-Client-Id: $CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID" -H "CF-Access-Client-Secret: $CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET"

claim: Portainer stack/service redeploy webhooks are POST endpoints under /api/stacks/webhooks/<uuid>; they support a `rollout-restart` query parameter and (for K8s) require Portainer Business Edition and a Git-deployed application.
quote: https://portainer:9443/api/stacks/webhooks/40ac1662-47c3-4a8e-b148-2a34eb52bb42 ... rollout-restart=all ... Webhooks require Portainer Business Edition

claim: Watchtower polls the registry on a configurable interval (`--interval`/`WATCHTOWER_POLL_INTERVAL`, default 300s) and recreates any container whose image digest has changed, so it can act as a no-CI-credential pull agent for tags like `pr-123` once images are pushed to GHCR.
quote: The --interval parameter controls how frequently watchtower will poll for new images, defaulting to 300 seconds (5 minutes).

claim: A 2026 walk-through documents the canonical Portainer-webhook-from-Actions pipeline: build & push to a registry, then a final job step calls the per-stack webhook URL stored in repo secrets; the post calls out 're-pull image' and 'force redeployment' as the toggles to flip on the Portainer side for ephemeral previews.
quote: GitHub Actions builds and pushes the Docker image, then triggers Portainer to pull the new image and redeploy the container ... Re-pull image ... Force redeployment

claim: DSM 7.2's Container Manager exposes a Project tab that creates and operates multi-container Docker Compose stacks directly from the GUI.
quote: The Project dashboard makes it possible to create, operate, and manage multi-container Docker projects using Compose files.

claim: Third-party CLIs such as syno-docker exist specifically because Container Manager's Project UI is opinionated and scripting compose stacks per-PR is normally done over SSH.
quote: CLI tool for deploying Docker containers to Synology DSM 7.2+

claim: Portainer stack webhooks fire a redeploy on a single POST and are intended for CI pipelines, but the documentation flags them as Business-Edition-only on non-Edge environments.
quote: This functionality is only available in Portainer Business Edition... only available on non-Edge environments

claim: Because stack webhooks are paywalled in CE, the community maintains an OSS sidecar (portainer-stack-webhook) that re-implements automatic stack updates for free.
quote: Free alternative to Portainer BE's "Automatic Stack Updates"

claim: Portainer CE remains free under zlib but the GitOps webhook auto-update path is reserved for Business Edition.
quote: Portainer BE extends the basic functionality that CE provides, leveraging the GitOps approach to allow for automatic updates of your applications

claim: Portainer installs cleanly on DSM 7.2 by deploying it as a container under Container Manager, exposing the full Docker feature set the native UI hides.
quote: Using Portainer offers access to all Docker features, not just the limited features set currently provided by Synology Container Manager.

claim: Dokploy sits at roughly 33.6k GitHub stars (April 2026) and is positioned as an open-source Vercel/Heroku alternative.
quote: Open Source Alternative to Vercel, Netlify and Heroku.

claim: Dokploy v0.28+ breaks on Synology DS920+ with 'client version 1.53 is too new. Maximum supported API version is 1.43' due to DSM's older Docker daemon, while v0.27.1 worked.
quote: client version 1.53 is too new. Maximum supported API version is 1.43

claim: Dokploy relies on Docker Swarm for multi-node orchestration, which is awkward on DSM since Synology does not bless Swarm and the bundled Docker is typically lagging.
quote: Dokploy stands out for its intuitive UI, robust Docker integration, and multi-server support via Docker Swarm.

claim: Coolify is around 54.3k GitHub stars (April 2026) with v4.0.0 released 2026-04-27.
quote: an open-source & self-hostable alternative to Heroku / Netlify / Vercel

claim: The Coolify install script fails on Synology with 'grep: /etc/os-release: No such file or directory'; the maintainer-blessed workaround is to run Coolify inside an Ubuntu VM on VMM, not directly on DSM.
quote: grep: /etc/os-release: No such file or directory

claim: Running k3s directly on DSM requires cross-compiling iptables kernel modules, loading them with insmod (modprobe is absent), switching containerd to the 'native' snapshotter (no overlay), adding a static cni0 route, and disabling DSM's firewall.
quote: Synology does not have the overlay snapshotter kernel module

claim: Even after kernel-module hacks, k3s on Synology hits cgroup-support-not-found failures on units like the DS220+, confirming bare-metal k3s on DSM is fragile.
quote: unable to start k3s agent - cgroup support not found - synology ds220plus

claim: Argo CD's ApplicationSet Pull Request generator queries GitHub/GitLab/Gitea/Bitbucket APIs to auto-create one Application per open PR and tear it down on merge — the canonical k3s preview pattern.
quote: The Pull Request generator uses the API of an SCMaaS provider (GitHub, Gitea, or Bitbucket Server) to automatically discover open pull requests within a repository.

claim: The pragmatic homelab pattern is to keep k3s nodes on separate Linux VMs and let Synology serve only as storage via the open-source Synology CSI plugin.
quote: Synology CSI plugin for Kubernetes

claim: Real-world write-up on running a self-hosted GitHub Actions runner directly on a Synology NAS, calling it 'unlimited automation, zero cloud queueing, and no surprise bills' but flagging that the package only supports organization-level (not per-repo) runners.
quote: self-hosted GitHub Actions runner, working for you, right at home... currently supports only organization-level runners, not per-repository runners

claim: March 2026 critique: Synology Container Manager's latest 2025 release ships Docker Engine 24.0.2, unsupported since June 2024, and users 'frequently run into issues with permissions, even within my Docker volume, as well as inaccurate resource monitoring'; recommends Portainer/Dockge/Dockhand instead.
quote: Its latest version, released in 2025, uses Docker Engine 24.0.2, which has been unsupported since June 2024... I frequently run into issues with permissions, even within my Docker volume, as well as inaccurate resource monitoring.

claim: October 2025 issue from telnetdoogie documenting that the DSM 7.3 -> 7.3.1 update silently deletes downloaded kernel modules if Docker was previously updated, causing containers to fail at startup until install_iptables_modules.sh is re-run.
quote: DSM 7.3.1 May break your updated Docker!! How to fix...

claim: SynoForum thread (March 2025) where a user reports BTRFS snapshots accumulating in the @docker directory consuming 4.7TB of a 10.5TB volume; aggressive cleanup scripts have removed 148 subvolumes and broken every container on the box.
quote: All snapshots end up accumulating in the @docker directory... 4.7TB worth of snapshots on a 10.5TB volume

claim: Synology's reverse-proxy nginx.conf is regenerated from .mustache templates on every DSM update, wiping hand-edited vhosts; the only workaround the community has found is dropping configs into /usr/local/etc/nginx/sites-enabled, which survives but is invisible in the DSM UI.
quote: the location /usr/local/etc/nginx/sites-enabled for custom 'survive-the-DSM-update' reverse proxy configurations that don't show in the UI but still work... nginx.conf gets recreated by the NAS based on .mustache files

claim: Public homelab project explicitly built around Cloudflare Tunnel + Synology to expose multiple subdomains (nas.mydomain.net -> 192.168.1.200:5000) without port forwarding, designed for consumer ISPs including CGNAT-affected ones like Xfinity.
quote: Use Cloudflare Tunnels with a Synology Router and NAS to host multiple home services on subdomains... without traditional port forwarding. This setup works on consumer ISPs (like Xfinity)

claim: Tailscale Funnel does not support wildcard subdomains and is limited to one domain per device, making it a poor fit for unbounded *.preview.example.com URLs unless you reverse-proxy a single funnel hostname through DSM's reverse proxy.
quote: Accessing wildcard subdomains with Tailscale Funnel is not currently supported... users are currently limited to one domain per device

claim: Marius Hosting's standard Synology cleanup recipe (updated Jan 2026) wires a dockerprune.sh script into DSM Task Scheduler to remove 'all stopped containers, unused volumes and container images', but the script cannot remove active containers and gives no quantified reclamation figure.
quote: all stopped containers, unused volumes and container images from your Docker that you are no longer using... will free up disk space on your NAS

claim: Synology removed the i915 graphics driver from DSM in the 2025 'x25' line (DS225+/DS425+), blocking Plex/Jellyfin hardware transcoding; if Plex shares the box with previews, software transcoding now eats most of the CPU budget on these models.
quote: Synology removed the i915 graphics driver from DSM, effectively disabling hardware transcoding on the Intel Celeron J4125 CPU... Both H.264 (AVC) and H.265 (HEVC) transcoding had been deliberately blocked at the kernel driver level.

claim: Homelab decision-framework post argues a $5/mo VPS or a free Cloudflare Pages tier beats a NAS for any preview workload that needs to be reachable on the public internet, with the NAS reserved for storage/backup and a mini PC handling Docker workloads.
quote: NAS, Mini PC, or VPS (FREE!). Which one?

claim: The most cited Synology + GitHub Actions blog post deploys a single container to the NAS via Tailscale and a webhook — it does not branch on PR number, allocate per-PR URLs, or tear anything down on close.
quote: How to deploy an application from Github Actions to your Synology NAS — push docker containers built using GitHub Actions directly to your Synology without any human interaction, using Tailscale (built on top of WireGuard) and a webhook container.

claim: PullPreview, the closest off-the-shelf 'review apps on your own infra' GitHub Action, supports exactly two providers and explicitly does not target a generic SSH host or NAS.
quote: PullPreview supports two providers: lightsail [and] hetzner … The page does not mention support for generic SSH servers, bring-your-own-VM solutions, self-hosted hardware, or services like Synology NAS.

claim: PullPreview's marketing reinforces this: it provisions VMs in your AWS/Hetzner account, not on hardware you already own — so users with a Synology can't drop it in.
quote: PullPreview supports Lightsail and Hetzner while keeping your code and infrastructure under your control … Lightsail AWS servers start at 3.5$/month for 512MB RAM, with the default instance type being 2GB at 10$/month.

claim: When developers ask publicly how to recreate Vercel-style PR previews on infrastructure they own, the answers default to vendor-managed offerings (Static Web Apps, deployment slots) — no self-hosted recipe is offered.
quote: automatic deployment of each pull request to a separate, isolated environment … unique URL for each PR (e.g., pr-123.ourapp.azurewebsites.net) … automatic cleanup when PRs are closed or merged … The answer does not mention any self-hosted or open-source solutions.

claim: Even purpose-built 'PR preview' projects on GitHub frame the gap explicitly — every team wants this, nobody wants to wire it up themselves — and ship as managed services rather than NAS-friendly recipes.
quote: every team wants preview environments but nobody wants to build them … Every pull request gets a complete production clone with frontend, backend, database seeded with test data, isolated network, custom subdomain, and SSL, and is torn down automatically on merge.

claim: The PullPreview author's own walk-through targets AWS, confirming there is no published 'PullPreview on a Synology' or equivalent end-to-end recipe in the indexable web.
quote: Automated preview deployments from GitHub to AWS in 10 minutes — once installed in your repository, this action is triggered whenever a change is made to pull requests labeled with pullpreview, continuously deploys updates from pull requests, and reports preview URLs in the GitHub UI.
